en.extract:
The little slaty flycatcher (Ficedula basilanica) is a species of bird in the family Muscicapidae.
It is found on the islands of Mindanao, Leyte and Samar in the Philippines.
Its natural habitat is tropical moist lowland forests.
It is threatened by habitat loss.


Description and taxonomy
They are sexually dimorphic in which males have the eponymous slaty-grey color with the females being light brown. Usually secretive and solitary remaining hidden in low and dense foliage. It is known to feed on insects but otherwise diet is largely unknown.


Subspecies
Two subspecies are recognized:

F. b. basilanica - Found on Mindanao, Dinagat and Basilan. No recent records on Basilan and Dinagat Islands. Smaller ear patch, lighter head and a darker tail.
F. b. samarensis - Found on Samar and Leyte. No recent records on Leyte. Has a much more prominent white ear patch, a darker head and a paler tail.


Ecology and behavior

Its diet mostly unknown but it includes small invertebrates. It is usually solitary and typically found in dense undergrowth. This bird's breeding habits are poorly known. Birds in breeding condition with enlarged gonads have been collected in May and June. One nest has been found made of roots and leaves containing 2 eggs. No other information is known.


Habitat and conservation status
Its natural habitats at tropical moist lowland primary forest and well developed secondary forest up to 1,200 meters above sea level. It is often seen close to the forest floor staying in the low understory.
IUCN has assessed this bird as vulnerable with the population being estimated at 2,500 to 9,999 mature individuals. Extensive lowland deforestation on all islands in its range is the main threat. Most remaining lowland forest that is not afforded protection leaving it vulnerable to both legal and Illegal logging, conversion into farmlands through Slash-and-burn and mining. Most Ebird (approximately two-thirds) and sight observations are in the PICOP Logging Concession in Agusan del Sur and Surigao del Sur, since the closure of PICOP, illegal settlers have massively deforested the area for illegal hardwoods and have been replanting cleared areas with paper pulp trees which do cannot support these birds. 
It occurs in the protected areas of Pasonanca Natural Park, Mount Apo National Park and Mt. Hilong-hilong National Park but enforcement and protection from loggers are lax.


References


External links

Image at ADW

The '''little slaty flycatcher''' ('''''Ficedula basilanica''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Muscicapidae]].
It is found on the islands of [[Mindanao]], [[Leyte]] and [[Samar]] in the [[Philippines]].

Its natural [[habitat]] is tropical moist lowland [[forest]]s.
It is threatened by [[habitat loss]].

== Description and taxonomy ==
They are sexually dimorphic in which males have the eponymous slaty-grey color with the females being light brown.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Allen|first=Desmond|title=Birds of the Philippines|publisher=Lynx and Birdlife International Fieldguides|year=2020|location=Barcelona|pages=332–333}}</ref> Usually secretive and solitary remaining hidden in low and dense foliage. It is known to feed on insects but otherwise diet is largely unknown.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Clement |first=Peter |date=2020 |title=Little Slaty Flycatcher (Ficedula basilanica), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/lisfly1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.lisfly1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

=== Subspecies ===
Two [[subspecies]] are recognized:

* ''F. b. basilanica'' - Found on [[Mindanao]], [[Dinagat Islands|Dinagat]] and [[Basilan]]. No recent records on Basilan and Dinagat Islands. Smaller ear patch, lighter head and a darker tail.
* ''F. b. samarensis'' - Found on [[Samar]] and [[Leyte]]. No recent records on Leyte. Has a much more prominent white ear patch, a darker head and a paler tail.<ref name=":0" />

== Ecology and behavior ==
[[File:Little Slaty Flycatcher (cropped).jpg|left|thumb|A male of the subspecies ''F. b. samarensis''.]]
Its diet mostly unknown but it includes small invertebrates. It is usually solitary and typically found in dense undergrowth. This bird's breeding habits are poorly known. Birds in breeding condition with enlarged gonads have been collected in May and June. One nest has been found made of roots and leaves containing 2 eggs. No other information is known.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Clement |first=Peter |date=2020 |title=Little Slaty Flycatcher (Ficedula basilanica), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/lisfly1/1.0/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.lisfly1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Habitat and conservation status ==
Its natural [[habitat]]s at [[Subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest|tropical moist lowland]] [[primary forest]] and well developed [[secondary forest]] up to 1,200 meters above sea level. It is often seen close to the [[forest floor]] staying in the low [[understory]].

IUCN has assessed this bird as [[Vulnerable species|vulnerable]] with the population being estimated at 2,500 to 9,999 mature individuals. Extensive lowland deforestation on all islands in its range is the main threat. Most remaining lowland forest that is not afforded protection leaving it vulnerable to both legal and [[Illegal logging]], conversion into farmlands through [[Slash-and-burn]] and mining. Most Ebird (approximately two-thirds) and sight observations are in the PICOP Logging Concession in [[Agusan del Sur]] and [[Surigao del Sur]], since the closure of PICOP, illegal settlers have massively deforested the area for illegal hardwoods and have been replanting cleared areas with paper pulp trees which do cannot support these birds. 

It occurs in the protected areas of [[Pasonanca Natural Park]], [[Mount Apo|Mount Apo National Park]] and Mt. Hilong-hilong National Park but enforcement and protection from loggers are lax.
